data.title: U.S. crude stockpiles fell 1.8M barrels last week, API says
data.description:
The American Petroleum Institute reportedly shows a larger-than-expected draw of 1.79M barrels of oil in U.S. commercial stockpiles for the week ending April 24, after declining by 4.4M barrels in the previous week.

Gasoline inventories reportedly show a draw of 8.47M barrels for the week, distillate inventories reportedly show a draw of 2.6M barrels, and inventories at the Cushing storage hub reportedly show a draw of 820K barrels.

The Energy Information Administration is scheduled to release its weekly U.S. petroleum supply report on Wednesday; the average of analysts surveyed by The Wall Street Journal forecast domestic commercial crude stocks would decline by 100K barrels, gasoline inventories were expected to drop by 2.1M barrels, and distillate inventories were expected to decline by 1.8M barrels.

WTI front-month June crude oil (CL1:COM) recently traded at $99.62/bbl, down from Tuesday's $99.93/bbl settlement price.

ETF: (USO)

data.title: Huntington Ingalls wins $282.9M contract for Navy frigate support work
data.description:
• Huntington Ingalls (HII) has been awarded a $282.9M cost-plus award fee contract for support work on the Navy’s FF(X) class frigate program.
  • The contract involves lead yard support for frigate design and development activities.
  • The work is expected to be completed by April 2028.
  • Around $59.1M (73%) in shipbuilding funds and $21.5M (27%) in R&D funds for FY2026 will be used at award, and the contract is managed by Naval Sea Systems Command.

data.title: Camden National anticipates 2 to 5 bps of core net interest margin expansion in Q2 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Camden National Corporation (CAC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Earlier this morning, we reported strong first quarter results with net income of $21.9 million and earnings per share of $1.29,” said Simon Griffiths (CEO, President & Director), adding that “adjusted net income and adjusted diluted EPS increased 39% year-over-year in the first quarter of 2026.”
  • Griffiths framed Q1 performance as steady versus Q4: “These results were near our record earnings reported last quarter,” and said the quarter reflected “the continued value generated by the Northway Financial acquisition and ongoing organic financial improvements across the franchise, despite macroeconomic headwinds and the seasonal softening we typically experience in the first quarter.”
  • On balance sheet and capital, Griffiths said, “Our tangible common equity ratio increasing to 7.64% at quarter's end,” and added, “we are focused on balancing reinvestment in the franchise with returning capital to shareholders, including through our recently announced share repurchase program and regular cash dividend.”
  • On funding, Griffiths said, “Our deposit base reached $5.6 billion at March 31, representing a 1% increase from the prior quarter,” while emphasizing, “Our goal is to build long-term customer relationships, not simply pursue rate-driven volume.”
  • On product/technology, Griffiths said, “Our internally developed AI platform, Camden IQ, anchors our AI initiatives,” and highlighted new tools: “Recently, we launched Prep IQ,” and “Loan IQ, another internally developed tool, further enhances efficiency by streamlining access to loan policy.”
  • Michael Archer (Executive VP & CFO) tied profitability to operating discipline: “Our financial operating metrics continue to trend favorably,” citing “a reported return on average assets of 1.28%, a return on average tangible equity of 18.17%, and a non-GAAP efficiency ratio of 53.21%.”

Outlook

  • Archer guided near-term margin: “As we move into the second quarter, we anticipate net interest margin expansion of 2 to 5 basis points on a core basis,” while adding, “Our current interest rate outlook calls for a slower and more gradual net interest margin expansion throughout 2026 as the likelihood of further Fed rate cuts has decreased.”
  • Archer guided fee recovery: “As we move into the second quarter, we anticipate noninterest income to rebound to approximately $13 million.”
  • Archer guided expenses: “We are currently estimating a noninterest expense of approximately $37.5 million for the second quarter.”
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, management moved from Q1 expectations of “a couple of basis points” of core margin expansion (Q4 2025 call) to a Q2 2026 view of “2 to 5 basis points on a core basis,” and Archer also shifted rate-cut framing from “maybe some -- a couple of Fed rate cuts here in the future” (Q4 2025 call) to “the likelihood of further Fed rate cuts has decreased” (Q1 2026 call).

Financial Results

  • Archer said, “For the first quarter, we reported a net interest margin of 3.24%,” explaining it was “up 20 basis points year-over-year and down 5 basis points from the previous quarter,” with the linked-quarter decline “driven by lower fair value mark accretion income of $956,000.”
  • Archer separated core trends from purchase accounting: “Our underlying core net interest margin remained stable at 2.92% between periods.”
  • Archer detailed fee and wealth metrics: “Assets under administration across our wealth and brokerage business remained essentially flat during the first quarter and were $2.4 billion at March 31,” and added, “we are pleased to see AUA grow 11% year-over-year and quarterly revenues continuing to grow.”
  • Archer cited cost performance and normalization: “Noninterest expenses totaled $35.7 million in the first quarter, down 3% from the previous quarter,” and said Q2 would normalize due to “the true-up of our incentive accrual bon payout in the first quarter” and “our annual merit cycle and other seasonal costs.”
  • On credit, Archer said, “Nonperforming loans were just 22 basis points of total loans and past-due loans were just 6 basis points of total loans,” and added, “Net charge-offs for the quarter totaled $506,000,” with “our first quarter provision expense of $553,000.”
  • On capital return and book value, Archer said, “Our tangible book value per share grew 3% to $30.58 at March 31,” and added, “Through regular cash dividends and share repurchases, the company returned $8.6 million in capital to its shareholders.”

Q&A

  • Damon Del Monte, KBW: Asked what drives “2 to 5 basis points of core expansion”; Michael Archer (Executive VP & CFO): “primarily on the liability side,” citing deposit seasonality, CDs repricing, and that “as we get into the back half, we'll start to see some benefit” as “some of our derivatives start to roll off.”
  • Damon Del Monte, KBW: Asked about accretion “like $4.5 million or so”; Archer: “overall, I think we're about $4.3 million for the quarter,” adding “$4.5 million, maybe a little bit north of that is still a pretty good run rate estimate.”
  • Damon Del Monte, KBW: Asked loan growth outlook and suggested “low to mid-single digit”; Simon Griffiths (CEO, President & Director): “Yes. That feels reasonable.”
  • Stephen Moss, Raymond James: Asked if New Hampshire hiring could lift expenses; Griffiths: “we continue to be extremely disciplined,” adding, “we don't see a material impact to the expense side.”
  • Stephen Moss, Raymond James: Asked commercial pricing; Archer: “deals kind of in that 6% to low sixes on average,” while saying Camden would “maintain our discipline.”
  • Stephen Moss, Raymond James: Asked M&A posture; Griffiths: “we don't feel pressure to make a deal,” and said Camden is “certainly looking” but “not going to overreach.”
  • Matthew Breese, Stephens: Asked where liability-side margin improvement comes from; Archer pointed to deposit remix and said some derivatives “are a little bit underwater,” adding, “there could be an opportunity where we start approaching 3% on a margin -- core margin basis.”
  • Matthew Breese, Stephens: Asked resi mix and growth; Archer: “we're generally plus or minus 50-50,” and said resi would be “slower growth and more relationship-based growth.”
  • Matthew Breese, Stephens: Asked buyback cadence; Archer: “we'll continue to be opportunistic,” and said repurchases depend “in large part on our share price.”
  • Daniel Cardenas, Brean Capital: Asked whether competition is intensifying; Griffiths: “we definitely felt a pickup in competition over the last 3, 6 months,” citing “pricing pressure.”
  • Daniel Cardenas, Brean Capital: Asked line utilization; Archer: “we're kind of in that 35%, 40% neighborhood.”
  • Daniel Cardenas, Brean Capital: Asked if mid-single-digit fee income growth is achievable; Archer: “Yes, I think that's fair.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts sentiment was slightly negative to neutral, with repeated probing on margin drivers, competitive intensity, and sustainability of loan growth (e.g., Del Monte’s focus on margin mechanics and Breese’s drilling into funding levers and buybacks).
  • Management sentiment was slightly positive, with confidence rooted in capital, pipelines, and operating discipline, including Griffiths’ “we believe we are well positioned for the remainder of 2026,” and Archer’s “we feel pretty good” around margin expansion.
  • Compared with the prior quarter, management language was more tempered on rates, shifting from Q4’s openness to “a couple of Fed rate cuts” to Q1’s view that “the likelihood of further Fed rate cuts has decreased.”

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The prepared remarks shifted from Q4’s “record-setting” framing to Q1 emphasizing resilience “despite macroeconomic headwinds and the seasonal softening we typically experience in the first quarter.”
  • Product emphasis rotated from Q4’s customer-facing digital products and automation metrics to Q1’s banker productivity tooling, with Griffiths highlighting “Camden IQ,” “Prep IQ,” and “Loan IQ.”
  • Analysts stayed focused on margin and growth in both quarters, but Q1 featured more direct questions on competition (“pickup in competition”) and capital deployment via repurchases.

Risks and Concerns

  • Griffiths cited “macroeconomic headwinds” and said loan growth was “tempered this quarter” due to “typical seasonality.”
  • Griffiths acknowledged competitive pressure: “we definitely felt a pickup in competition over the last 3, 6 months,” tying it to “pricing pressure.”
  • Archer flagged purchase-accounting variability as a driver of reported margin, noting the linked-quarter change was “driven by lower fair value mark accretion income.”

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a strong but seasonally softer quarter, highlighted by $21.9 million in net income and $1.29 in EPS, stable core margin, low problem assets, and continued capital rebuilding post-Northway. For Q2, management pointed investors to expected core margin expansion of 2 to 5 basis points, noninterest income around $13 million, and noninterest expense around $37.5 million, while acknowledging intensified competition and a reduced expectation for additional Fed rate cuts.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Silicon Motion Technology Non-GAAP EPS of $1.58 beats by $0.28, revenue of $342.1M beats by $42.52M
data.description:

data.title: Ventas expects $3.86 FFO per share in 2026 as it raises investment plan to $3B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ventas, Inc. (VTR) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Ventas delivered 9% year-over-year growth in total same-store property NOI and normalized FFO per share" and "SHOP NOI grew over 15% and U.S. occupancy increased 370 basis points" (Chairman & CEO Debra Cafaro).
  • "Based on our first quarter results and our confidence, we have improved our outlook for the full year, increasing our midpoint guidance for FFO per share by $0.03 to $3.86 per share" and "we have now increased our 2026 investment volume guidance to $3 billion" (Chairman & CEO Cafaro).
  • "Same-store average occupancy increased 310 basis points year-over-year, reaching 90.4%" and "NOI margins expanding 170 basis points year-over-year to 30% and incremental margins at 50%" (Executive VP of Senior Housing & Chief Investment Officer J. Hutchens).
  • "Earlier this month, we completed a $540 million acquisition of the Revel portfolio" and "excluding the Revel transaction, our remaining senior housing investments completed so far in 2026 are expected to generate a 6.9% year 1 NOI yield" (Executive VP Hutchens).
  • "Normalized FFO for the first quarter was $0.94 per share" and "liquidity is strong with $5.5 billion available at the end of the first quarter" (Executive VP & CFO Robert Probst).
  • "Pete is retiring after an extraordinary 4-decade career in commercial real estate and 8 years leading our OMAR business" (Chairman & CEO Cafaro).

Outlook

  • "We now expect normalized FFO per share to range from $3.82 to $3.89 or $3.86 at the midpoint" (Executive VP & CFO Probst).
  • "We are increasing our total company same-store cash NOI growth outlook to nearly 10% at the midpoint" and "increase our 2026 SHOP outlook, including same-store NOI growth of 16% at the midpoint, which is up from 15%" (Executive VP & CFO Probst).
  • "This is driven by a higher expectation of occupancy growth of approximately 300 basis points" and "our success during the key selling season will determine the full year outcome" (Executive VP Hutchens).
  • "We are increasing our senior housing focused investment guidance from $2.5 billion to $3 billion for 2026" (Executive VP Hutchens).

Financial Results

  • "Our Outpatient Medical & Research portfolio, or OMAR, delivered 2.4% same-store cash NOI growth" and "outpatient medical growing 3.1% year-over-year" (Executive VP & CFO Probst).
  • "Our triple-net segment grew same-store cash NOI by 1.6% in the quarter, benefiting from the 35% Brookdale cash rent escalator" (Executive VP & CFO Probst).
  • "Net debt-to-EBITDA improved to 5x at quarter end" and "we raised approximately $2.4 billion of equity designated for 2026 investment activity" (Executive VP & CFO Probst).

Q&A

  • Julien Blouin, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: "what has sort of driven the underperformance of that portfolio"; Executive VP Hutchens: "they used third-party management" then "set up Revel and that was a slow start" and Ventas is "seeing pretty immediate sales upside."
  • Julien Blouin, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: "are you starting to see that reflected in some of the cap rates"; Executive VP Hutchens: "there's a drift down in cap rates from the 7s and into the 6s" and "we're expecting high 6s moving forward."
  • James Kammert, Evercore ISI Institutional Equities, Research Division: asked about expense drivers; Executive VP Hutchens: "total expenses 5.8%" and "a lot of it was weather-related."
  • Nicholas Joseph, Citigroup Inc., Research Division: asked when capital moves into development; Executive VP Hutchens: "we're still 20% to 40% off in terms of where rents need to be" for development to work.
  • Vikram Malhotra, Mizuho Securities USA LLC, Research Division: asked about Ventas expanding funds; Chairman & CEO Cafaro: "We do have a Ventas investment management business" and "we're well positioned to continue to try to expand" via "additional vehicles."
  • Michael Goldsmith, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division: asked on Brookdale transition upside; Executive VP Hutchens: "we really viewed '26 as the year to put all the pieces in place, and then '27 is -- and beyond is really the NOI growth opportunity."
  • Nicholas Yulico, Scotiabank Global Banking and Markets, Research Division: asked about non-SHOP portfolio strategy; Chairman & CEO Cafaro: "the focus is on basically growing SHOP organically and externally" and "the other parts of the portfolio are becoming a smaller portion."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative/pressing, with repeated probing on competition and pricing power (e.g., "are you starting to see that reflected in some of the cap rates?" (Julien Blouin, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division) and "at what point are you starting to see any of that capital... move into development" (Nicholas Joseph, Citigroup Inc., Research Division)).
  • Management: positive/confident in prepared remarks and steady in Q&A, emphasizing execution and visibility (e.g., "we have improved our outlook for the full year" (Chairman & CEO Cafaro) and "our win rate has been pretty consistent" (Executive VP Hutchens)).
  • Versus last quarter: management again leaned into the multiyear senior housing setup, but the current call added more deal-specific scrutiny (Revel lease-up) and more discussion of cap-rate compression, while guidance was raised rather than initiated.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • "increasing our midpoint guidance for FFO per share by $0.03 to $3.86 per share" (Chairman & CEO Cafaro) followed Q4’s initial 2026 midpoint of "$3.83" (Executive VP & CFO Probst).
  • "same-store NOI growth of 16% at the midpoint, which is up from 15%" (Executive VP Hutchens) compared with Q4’s "15% at the midpoint" SHOP outlook (Executive VP Hutchens).
  • "increasing our senior housing focused investment guidance from $2.5 billion to $3 billion" (Executive VP Hutchens) compared with Q4’s "guidance of $2.5 billion of investments" (Executive VP Hutchens).
  • Q&A focus shifted from winter/flu and guidance building blocks (Q4) to underwriting and execution on a large value-add lease-up (Revel), plus cap-rate drift and competitive intensity (Q1).

Risks and Concerns

  • "our success during the key selling season will determine the full year outcome" (Executive VP Hutchens).
  • "operating expenses increased 5.8% year-over-year" and management cited "winter storm-related costs" (Executive VP Hutchens).
  • "partially offset by $0.01 from the higher forward interest rate curve" (Executive VP & CFO Probst).
  • "there is more interest in the sector" and "cap rates have drifted down a bit" (Executive VP Hutchens).
  • On development risk, management said supply still doesn’t pencil: "we're still 20% to 40% off" (Executive VP Hutchens).

Final Takeaway

Ventas management framed Q1 as an inflection period for senior housing demand, pointing to double-digit SHOP growth, raised full-year normalized FFO guidance to a $3.86 midpoint, and increased 2026 investment volume guidance to $3 billion. Leadership highlighted balance-sheet flexibility and equity funding for acquisitions, while Q&A centered on executing the $540 million Revel lease-up and navigating a more competitive transaction market alongside weather costs, interest-rate headwinds, and reliance on the May–September selling season.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Polaris expects Q2 sales growth of 5% to 7% and adjusted EPS of $0.70 to $0.80 while targeting China sourcing below 5% by end of 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Polaris (PII) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered a strong start to the year" and "results exceeded our expectations during the quarter, with reported sales up 8% or up 14% organically, excluding Indian Motorcycle and its related impacts," said CEO Michael Speetzen. "We delivered adjusted EPS of $0.13," and he added that "EPS, excluding Indian would have been $0.26."

  • CEO Speetzen said first-quarter demand was led by utility and commercial: "First quarter sales were driven by double-digit growth in our Power Sports segment. led by our utility RANGER line, our fast-growing commercial business and Snowmobiles." He also highlighted retail and share: "North American retail grew 1% with ORV up 3%," and "we ended the quarter with share gains in both ORV and Snow as well as with Godfrey pontoons."

  • CEO Speetzen tied margin expansion to mix, price, and efficiency despite tariffs: "Our margins, even with 240 basis points of headwind from tariffs, we're able to improve gross margins by 389 basis points." He also said dealer channel health remained intact: "Dealer inventory levels remain healthy," and snow inventory improved: "we made significant progress on snowmobile inventory and ended the quarter down over 50% from a year ago."

  • CFO Robert Mack described a key reporting change and segment drivers: "we are now reporting our business in 3 new segments: Polaris Powersports, Marine and Aixam & Goupil." He said sales and margin improved: "Sales were up 8% or approximately 14% organically when excluding Indian Motorcycle" and "Adjusted EBITDA margin improved by approximately 280 basis points," while noting "Tariffs also posed a 240 basis point headwind in the quarter."

Outlook

  • CFO Mack said Polaris is holding to the updated outlook: "We are reaffirming the guidance we updated on March 3 when we raised our outlook following the earlier-than-expected closure of the Indian Motorcycle separation." He framed the stance as conservative: "given the current level of uncertainty, we have decided to take a prudent and disciplined approach to the outlook."

  • For Q2, CFO Mack guided: "we expect sales to grow year-over-year in the range of 5% to 7%" and "Adjusted EPS is expected to be between $0.70 and $0.80." He added tariff expectations for the quarter and year: "we expect a negative year-over-year impact from tariffs to be between $30 million and $35 million" and "we continue to expect total tariff costs of approximately $215 million this year."

  • Management reiterated longer-run tariff mitigation goals: CFO Mack said Polaris aims "to reduce China source material cost of goods sold from 14% last year to below 5% by the end of 2027" and CEO Speetzen repeated, "we are also well underway with our goal to reduce China-sourced components to under 5% of material cost of goods sold by the end of 2027."

Financial Results

  • CEO Speetzen summarized the quarter’s topline and profitability: "reported sales up 8%" and "adjusted EPS of $0.13." He said tariff pressure was offset operationally: "even with 240 basis points of headwind from tariffs, we're able to improve gross margins by 389 basis points" and "Adjusted EBITDA margins increased 277 basis points."

  • CFO Mack provided segment-level performance highlights: "Sales in Polaris Powersports were up 14% year-over-year" and "PG&A was up 14%." He added profitability drivers: "Gross margin in the quarter was up 422 basis points, overcoming the anticipated significant headwind from tariffs due to strong mix, positive net price and operational efficiencies."

  • CFO Mack detailed below-segment items that affected results and cash: he said first-quarter OpEx timing was "about $30 million in the quarter" and described a one-time supplier cost: "there was a $22 million" charge tied to "the First Brands bankruptcy." On cash, he noted seasonality: "First quarter free cash flow is typically our weakest quarter" and "while a net outflow, our first quarter cash flow was better than we had planned."

Q&A

  • Craig Kennison, Baird: Asked to "unpack your tariff exposure" including IEEPA and Section 232; CEO Speetzen said "our tariff impact has stayed consistent with what we talked about at around $215 million" and that IEEPA/122 changes "yielded about a $40 million benefit" that was "effectively offset" by 232, while CFO Mack confirmed: "Correct. Yes."

  • James Hardiman, Citi: Pressed on tariff-driven competitive dynamics and pricing; CEO Speetzen said "there's not a tremendous amount of price elasticity in this market" and "I don't want to get into commentary about our competitors," while CFO Mack added, "I don't think it's going to dramatically change the overall competitive dynamic."

  • Hardiman, Citi: Asked whether stability in consumer/tariffs could mean upside; CEO Speetzen said, "if the consumer backdrop continues playing out like it has, and if tariff policy... were to just stay where it is today, we would have certainly been looking at taking guidance up," but flagged uncertainty around "what happens with tariffs" and consumer conditions.

  • Joseph Altobello, Raymond James: Asked about tariff run-rate into 2027 and OpEx timing; CEO Speetzen said mitigation and the China reduction "should be a net benefit," while CFO Mack quantified pull-forward OpEx: "it was about $30 million in the quarter" and said "it will turn around relatively evenly through the course of the year."

  • Noah Zatzkin, KeyBanc: Asked about margin drivers and a distressed supplier adjustment; CFO Mack said the largest gross margin driver was "volume and mix" followed by "net price" and then "plant performance," and explained the supplier item as "a one period -- one quarter impact" totaling "that $22 million."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts slightly negative: questions repeatedly centered on tariffs, uncertainty, and guidance conservatism, including "help us unpack your tariff exposure" (Kennison) and whether a stable scenario "equate[s] to upside" (Hardiman).

  • Management slightly positive but cautious: CEO Speetzen emphasized execution and product strength while repeatedly stressing uncertainty, including "we have decided to take a prudent and disciplined approach" (CFO Mack) and "there's just a tremendous amount of uncertainty around this" (CEO Speetzen).

  • Versus last quarter, tariffs remained the dominant framing, but management language shifted toward conservatism despite a stronger start, with CEO Speetzen describing the approach as "conservative" and "prudent" in Q1, compared with Q4’s emphasis on navigating a "challenging year" and the then-expected timing of the Indian separation.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Segment structure changed in Q1: CFO Mack said Polaris is "now reporting our business in 3 new segments," versus the prior-quarter discussion centered on Off-Road/On-Road/Marine and pending post-Indian changes.

  • Indian Motorcycle separation timing moved forward: in Q1, CFO Mack said guidance was updated after the "earlier-than-expected closure of the Indian Motorcycle separation," while in Q4 management said it "remains on track to close by the end of this quarter."

  • Guidance posture tightened: Q1 management repeatedly cited uncertainty as the reason not to raise outlook despite Q1 overperformance, while Q4 provided full-year 2026 guidance with assumptions and positioned 2026 as "playing offense."

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariff policy volatility remained the key risk: CEO Speetzen said, "there's a tremendous amount of uncertainty around this" and highlighted reviews of "301" and "USMCA," while CFO Mack warned about timing and policy cliffs: "we know the 122s have to -- they have to expire in July."

  • Consumer sensitivity, especially in recreation, was reiterated: CEO Speetzen said recreational categories are "incredibly sensitive to everything that's going on" and that the mid-March retail decline "correlated with increased geopolitical tensions and rising oil prices."

  • Supply chain disruption risk appeared via the supplier bankruptcy: CFO Mack said the supplier transition was necessary because Polaris "would have lost a lot of margin if we had lost supply," and described the actions taken to "keep inventory flowing."

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a stronger-than-expected start with "reported sales up 8%" and "adjusted EPS of $0.13," driven by utility-led powersports growth, improved mix, and operational efficiencies that expanded margins despite a "240 basis point" tariff headwind. Leadership reaffirmed its updated outlook and guided Q2 sales growth of "5% to 7%" with adjusted EPS of "$0.70 to $0.80," while emphasizing uncertainty in consumer demand and shifting tariff rules. The call repeatedly pointed to tariffs as the main swing factor—still expected at about "$215 million" for the year—alongside a multi-year mitigation plan to push China-sourced material costs to "below 5% by the end of 2027."

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Welltower trades higher after Q1 earnings beat estimates, 2026 guidance raised
data.description:
Welltower (WELL) was trading higher after the REIT reported first-quarter earnings that exceeded expectations, with the full-year guidance raised.

Shares were 0.43% higher at $215.15 during post-market trading on Tuesday.

The rental housing operator for aging seniors reported normalized FFO of $1.47 per diluted share, up 23% year-over-year and above the S&P Cap IQ consensus estimate of $1.44.

Same-store net operating income grew 16.4% on the back of a 22.1% growth in the seniors housing operating portfolio.

Revenue increased to $3.35B from $2.42B, above the consensus of $3.20B.

Net debt to consolidated enterprise value decreased to 8.8% as of March 31 from 10.8% as of December 31, 2025.

The Toledo, Ohio-based company raised its full-year 2026 normalized FFO per share guidance to a range of $6.21 to $6.35 from $6.09 to $6.25. The consensus estimate stands at $6.21.

Earnings conference call is scheduled for Wednesday, April 29, at 9:00 AM ET.

data.title: GridAI Technologies misses 10-K filing deadline; Nasdaq issues warning
data.description:
• GridAI Technologies (GRDX) announced on Tuesday that it has received a notice from Nasdaq (NDAQ) for non-compliance after failing to file its Form 10-K for the year ended Dec 31, 2025, within the required timeline.
  • The company now has 60 days (until June 22, 2026) to submit a compliance plan. If accepted, Nasdaq (NDAQ) may grant up to 180 additional days (until Oct. 12, 2026) to restore full compliance.
  • The delay is due to a complex accounting review linked to its recent acquisition of Grid AI, the company said. They also plan to file the report and fix the issue as soon as possible.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Axogen outlines at least $270M 2026 revenue on at least 20% growth amid Avance biologic channel transition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Axogen (AXGN) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “We delivered strong growth across all of our target markets” and “we are raising our full year guidance accordingly,” said President, CEO & Director Michael Dale, citing “revenue of $61.5 million” and “Adjusted EBITDA was $5.7 million,” alongside “continued progress expanding commercial insurance coverage and payment for Avance” and an “on-schedule expansion of our commercial organization.” (President, CEO & Director Michael Dale)
  • Dale highlighted payer actions post-BLA approval: “Cigna extended explicit broad coverage to Avance Nerve Graft… for its approximately 16 million members,” while “Elevance removed Avance from its experimental investigational list” but added utilization management criteria “limiting Avance usage to 5 to 25-millimeter nerve gaps.” (President, CEO & Director Dale)
  • “We reported revenue of $61.5 million” and “gross margin… 75.2%,” while “Beginning in the second quarter… we expect to see incremental product cost pressure,” CFO Lindsey Hartley said, adding the company “remain[s] on track to be free cash flow positive for the full year.” (Chief Financial Officer Lindsey Hartley)

Outlook

  • “We now expect full year 2026 revenue growth of at least 20% or total revenue of at least $270 million,” CFO Hartley said, while reiterating “Full year 2026 gross margin is expected to remain in the range of 74% to 76%” and the company “continue[s] to expect to be free cash flow positive for the full year 2026.” (Chief Financial Officer Hartley)
  • Management framed guidance as not relying on incremental reimbursement wins: “It does not assume material benefit from payer coverage decisions beyond what is already known.” (Chief Financial Officer Hartley)
  • Versus the prior quarter’s outlook, management raised 2026 revenue expectations from “at least 18%… or total revenue of at least $265.7 million” to “at least 20%… or total revenue of at least $270 million,” while keeping the gross margin range “74% to 76%” and free-cash-flow-positive expectation unchanged. (Chief Financial Officer Hartley)

Financial Results

  • Axogen reported “gross profit… $46.2 million” and “gross margin… 75.2%,” with Hartley emphasizing Q1 “reflects our pre-biologic launch cost structure as Avance biologic product has not yet entered commercial channel.” (Chief Financial Officer Hartley)
  • The company posted “Net loss… $19.6 million or $0.38 per share,” which included “a onetime loss of $16.8 million… upon the extinguishment of our debt facility in January,” while “Adjusted net income was $4.1 million or $0.07 per share.” (Chief Financial Officer Hartley)
  • Capital structure and liquidity updates included “cash, cash equivalents, restricted cash and investments totaled $103.6 million,” and “we completed an upsized public offering, raising $133.3 million in net proceeds and used $69.7 million to fully retire our term loan.” (Chief Financial Officer Hartley)

Q&A

  • Michael Sarcone, Jefferies: Asked why full-year growth implies deceleration after Q1; President, CEO & Director Dale said “there are sequential increases that we're going to be comparing against,” adding guidance reflects “a productivity growth expectation that we need to continue to execute against.”
  • Sarcone, Jefferies: Asked whether recent coverage wins are embedded in guidance and timing of impact; President, CEO & Director Dale said payer changes “need to be socialized,” and “it is not an event that happens immediately,” with more insight expected as “the next quarter rolls out.”
  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: Asked what drove Q1 acceleration and implications for the “LRP of 15% to 20%”; President, CEO & Director Dale said acceleration reflects “cumulative effects” across hiring and market development, and “we have not made any adjustments to our long-range plan.”
  • Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: Asked about prostate early signals and timing; President, CEO & Director Dale said feedback is “anecdotal… all positive,” with a more substantive read “on schedule… end — really beginning into third quarter.”
  • Michael Kratky, Leerink: Asked which prostate metrics matter; Chief Marketing Officer Jens Schroeder Kemp said sites are tracking “potency and continence” and also assessing whether nerve grafting affects “workflow.”
  • Kratky, Leerink: Asked about remaining uncovered lives and potential Aetna impact; Rick Ditto said Aetna coverage “would put us in the mid-90s” and “We expect something by the end of June.”
  • Jayson Bedford, Raymond James: Asked if BLA product is selling and any pricing/launch changes; President, CEO & Director Dale said “We are now selling BLA-produced product,” with “no change in the pricing structure.”
  • Caitlin Cronin, Canaccord: Asked if biologic transition changes hospital approval processes; President, CEO & Director Dale said “There's no change whatsoever… The transition is effectively invisible to the customer.”
  • Cronin, Canaccord: Asked about payer restrictions tied to RECON; Rick Ditto said Elevance’s gap-length preauthorization is “surgically inappropriate,” and the company expects “an update sometime hopefully by the end of this year or early next year.”
  • Anthony Petrone, Mizuho: Asked breast training conversion; President, CEO & Director Dale said breast conversion has been “above 75%.”
  • Frank Takkinen, Lake Street: Asked sales rep mix and hiring pace; President, CEO & Director Dale said “we have 26 breast reps,” “120 reps” in extremities, and “The answer… is yes” on potentially hiring ahead, subject to “quality control.”
  • Takkinen, Lake Street: Asked gross margin cadence with biologic transition; Chief Financial Officer Hartley said in Q2 the company expects “the lower end of that range” to see “the most pressure.”
  • David Turkaly, Citizens JMP: Asked if payers naming indications implies narrower coverage; Rick Ditto said the label is “quite broad” and “we don't expect any big hiccups,” but the team stays engaged if “we hear denials.”
  • Turkaly, Citizens JMP: Asked about M&A given cash; President, CEO & Director Dale said the company will fund “infrastructure development,” and “if we opportunistically see assets… we'll absolutely pay attention.”
  • H.C. Wainwright (Katie for Yi Chen): Asked what drives growth (new surgeons vs accounts/indications); President, CEO & Director Dale said growth is driven by “numbers of surgeons” and “productivity,” with account additions “primarily driven by… oromaxillofacial and breast.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly positive and detail-oriented, repeatedly pressing on guidance conservatism, payer timelines, and prostate proof points, including questions about implied “decel,” whether coverage wins are “baked in,” and “specific metrics” for prostate success.
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, emphasizing execution timing and uncertainty in payer adoption; President, CEO & Director Dale cautioned coverage updates “is not an event that happens immediately,” and characterized prostate feedback as “anecdotal… not data.”
  • Compared with the prior quarter, management language shifted toward greater confidence through a raised guide (“We are raising our full year guidance accordingly”), while maintaining similar caution on payer timing and prostate gating data, consistent with earlier comments that payer processes lack a guaranteed “schedule.” (President, CEO & Director Dale)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The most material change was the higher 2026 revenue outlook (now “at least $270 million” and “at least 20%” growth) versus the prior guide (“at least $265.7 million” and “at least 18%”), while gross margin guidance stayed “74% to 76%.” (Chief Financial Officer Hartley)
  • Payer narrative advanced from broad expectations of moving coverage beyond “above 65%” to concrete policy actions (Cigna’s “explicit broad coverage” and Elevance’s removal from “experimental investigational,” with a new gap-length restriction now being actively addressed). (President, CEO & Director Dale)
  • The biologic transition discussion moved from future-tense (“begin selling… in the second quarter of 2026”) to current commercialization (“We are now selling BLA-produced product”), while continuing to describe the customer experience as “effectively invisible.” (President, CEO & Director Dale)

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged payer policy frictions and timing risk, noting coverage wins “need to be socialized” and contracted locally before translating to utilization; for Elevance, the company is working to remove criteria it views as “surgically inappropriate.” (President, CEO & Director Dale; Rick Ditto)
  • Gross margin risk remains tied to the biologic cost transition: “Beginning in the second quarter… we expect to see incremental product cost pressure,” with Q2 expected “at the lower end” of the annual range. (Chief Financial Officer Hartley)
  • Prostate remains gated by data maturity: management said current feedback is “anecdotal” and decisions will wait for signals expected “beginning into third quarter.” (President, CEO & Director Dale)

Final Takeaway

Axogen management described Q1 as a broad-based commercial execution quarter, paired with a raised 2026 outlook to “at least $270 million” in revenue and “at least 20%” growth, while keeping gross margin guidance at “74% to 76%” amid expected biologic-related cost pressure. Leadership pointed to tangible payer movement after Avance’s BLA approval—most notably Cigna’s broad coverage—while acknowledging that reimbursement changes may take time to translate into utilization and that prostate market development remains dependent on maturing clinical signals later in 2026.
